nativeRange.habitat: Found singly or in pairs over silty flat sand patches and rubble substrates near outer reef edges and coral drop-offs, at depths ranging from 1 to 45 m.

diet.notes: Feeds primarily by sifting mouthfuls of sand to extract small invertebrates, crustaceans, copepods, amphipods, mysis shrimp, and brine shrimp. Has been documented accepting frozen mysis, bloodworms, and prepared carnivore foods. Tends to require frequent feeding — daily at minimum — and benefits from a well-established sandbed rich in microfauna.

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 7150x4767. Tack-sharp in-situ pair over sand showing the diagnostic pale body with two bold dark horizontal stripes and black first-dorsal spot; pair shot matches this species' monogamous pairing.
Rickard Zerpe, 2540x1905. Sharp full-body side profile of a single individual clearly showing the two dark longitudinal stripes on a pale body and the black blotch on the first dorsal fin.

summary: The Sleeper Railway Glider Goby (Valenciennea helsdingenii) is a sand-sifting saltwater goby recognized by its pale body and two bold black horizontal stripes. Generally considered reef-safe and peaceful, it tends to be highly aggressive toward others of its own kind unless kept as a mated pair.
careNotes:
The Sleeper Railway Glider Goby is generally considered an intermediate-level species despite being listed as 'beginner' or 'easy' by a number of hobby retailers. Its demanding feeding requirements and sensitivity to poor water quality — particularly ammonia and nitrite — make it less forgiving than its peaceful temperament might suggest.

The species feeds by taking mouthfuls of sand and sifting out small invertebrates, so a deep, fine-grained sandbed with a well-established population of copepods, amphipods, and other microfauna is commonly advised. Without an active, diverse sandbed community, the goby may gradually exhaust its food supply and decline. Supplemental feeding with frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, and prepared carnivore foods helps, but daily feeding is generally recommended.

This species tends to be highly aggressive toward conspecifics and toward similarly patterned gobies. It is best kept singly or as a confirmed bonded pair; two individuals introduced without an established bond are likely to fight. Outside its own kind, the Railway Glider Goby tends to be peaceful toward reef inhabitants and is broadly considered reef-safe, though its sand-sifting activity can periodically deposit sediment on low-lying corals — something worth considering when planning the aquascape.

The species has been reported to jump from open-top aquariums, and a tightly fitting lid is generally recommended.
breedingNotes: Has been documented forming monogamous mated pairs in the wild and is a mound-building species. Captive breeding information is not widely available in hobby sources. Has been known to spawn in aquariums, though successful larval rearing is reportedly difficult.
